idioms.definition: To do something suddenly without planning it; acting on an impulse
examples: She booked the trip to Tokyo on a whim and didn't regret it for a second.
On a whim, he decided to change his entire career path.
They bought the cottage on a whim, but it turned out to be a wonderful decision.
idioms.usageTip: Used to describe spontaneous actions with no prior planning. The word 'whim' means a sudden desire or idea. Compare with 'on the spur of the moment', which has a similar meaning but often emphasises the speed of the decision.

idioms.idiom: on the spur of the moment
idioms.definition: Done suddenly without planning; impulsively; because of an immediate feeling
examples: On the spur of the moment, she decided to quit her job and start her own business.
The proposal wasn't planned – he asked her on the spur of the moment.
We drove to the coast on the spur of the moment and it turned into a brilliant day.
idioms.usageTip: Often used to emphasise that a decision happened very quickly, often prompted by the situation or an emotion. It can describe positive spontaneity or recklessness depending on context.

idioms.definition: To want something very much; to be determined to have or do something
examples: She had set her heart on studying medicine since she was a child.
He's set his heart on buying that house, even though it's over budget.
The team had set their hearts on winning the championship and refused to give up.
idioms.usageTip: Implies strong emotional attachment to a goal or desire, not just a preference. Often used when someone is deeply committed to an outcome. Can also suggest vulnerability if the goal is not achieved.

idioms.definition: To start behaving in a better way; to make a fresh start and improve one's behaviour
examples: After the health scare, he decided to turn over a new leaf and exercise regularly.
She promised to turn over a new leaf at school after a difficult first term.
He's really turned over a new leaf – he's punctual, focused, and much more positive.
idioms.usageTip: Always refers to personal improvement or reform. The 'leaf' here is a page of a book – turning it over means starting fresh. Use with 'decide to', 'promise to', or when describing a genuine change in behaviour.

idioms.definition: No logical explanation; without a sensible reason or pattern
examples: There seems to be no rhyme or reason to how the timetable was organised.
The staff changes happened with no rhyme or reason – no one understood the logic.
His decisions appear to have no rhyme or reason; they change from day to day.
idioms.usageTip: Used when something seems completely arbitrary or unexplainable. Often appears in phrases like 'there is no rhyme or reason' or 'with no rhyme or reason'. Also found as 'without rhyme or reason'.

idioms.definition: The result of chance rather than planning or skill; what you get when outcomes are random
examples: Not everyone gets to work with the best team – it's the luck of the draw.
Which examiner you get on the day is purely the luck of the draw.
Some people are born into wealthy families; it's just the luck of the draw.
idioms.usageTip: Refers to situations where outcomes are determined by chance, like drawing lots. Implies there is little you can do to control the result. Often used to accept or explain an unfair or random outcome.

fillInExercises.sentence: We stopped at the village café ___ – we hadn't planned to, but we're glad we did.
options: on the spur of the moment
fillInExercises.explanation: 'On a whim' means doing something spontaneously, without prior planning or a particular reason.

fillInExercises.sentence: He proposed to her ___ – he hadn't even bought a ring, but it felt right.
options: on the spur of the moment
by the luck of the draw
fillInExercises.correct: on the spur of the moment
fillInExercises.explanation: 'On the spur of the moment' describes a sudden, unplanned action prompted by an immediate feeling or situation.

fillInExercises.sentence: She has ___ becoming a concert pianist ever since she heard her first live performance.
options: turned over a new leaf about
had no rhyme or reason for
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Set your heart on' means to want something very strongly and be emotionally committed to achieving it.

fillInExercises.sentence: He promised his family he would ___ after getting into trouble at work.
options: leave it to the luck of the draw
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Turn over a new leaf' means to start behaving better and make a genuine effort to change for the better.

fillInExercises.sentence: The way tasks are allocated in this office seems to have ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'No rhyme or reason' means there is no logical explanation or pattern to something.

fillInExercises.sentence: Which classroom you're assigned to is just ___; the system picks randomly.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'The luck of the draw' refers to outcomes determined by chance, like a random draw or lottery.

lines.speaker: Anya
lines.text: I heard you handed in your notice. Was it a planned decision?

lines.speaker: Dom
lines.text: Not really. I did it [on the spur of the moment|on the spur of the moment] after a really difficult meeting. Maybe a bit rash.

lines.speaker: Anya
lines.text: Wow. So what are your plans now?

lines.speaker: Dom
lines.text: I've [set my heart on|set your heart on] starting my own design studio. I've been thinking about it for years, actually.

lines.speaker: Anya
lines.text: That's a big step. Have you thought it through properly, or is this [on a whim|do sth on a whim]?

lines.speaker: Dom
lines.text: A bit of both! But I've also decided to [turn over a new leaf|turn over a new leaf] – better habits, earlier starts, proper financial planning.

lines.speaker: Anya
lines.text: Good for you. And if the timing doesn't work out perfectly, I suppose it's partly [the luck of the draw|the luck of the draw].

lines.speaker: Dom
lines.text: Exactly. But I refuse to make decisions with [no rhyme or reason|no rhyme or reason] anymore. I want everything to be more intentional going forward.

lines.speaker: Lena
lines.text: Have you made any resolutions this year?

lines.speaker: Ed
lines.text: Yes, I've decided to [turn over a new leaf|turn over a new leaf]. No more late nights and last-minute work.

lines.speaker: Lena
lines.text: That sounds very intentional. Did something happen to prompt it, or was it [on a whim|do sth on a whim]?

lines.speaker: Ed
lines.text: A bit [on the spur of the moment|on the spur of the moment], actually – I wrote them down on New Year's Eve without much thought.

lines.speaker: Lena
lines.text: And are they realistic? You seem to have [set your heart on|set your heart on] some quite ambitious goals.

lines.speaker: Ed
lines.text: I hope so. The way I've been living lately had [no rhyme or reason|no rhyme or reason] – just reacting to everything. I need some structure.

lines.speaker: Lena
lines.text: Well, whether it works is partly [the luck of the draw|the luck of the draw], but attitude makes a real difference too.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: to do something suddenly without planning it

pairs.idiom: on the spur of the moment

pairs.meaning: to want something very much; to be determined to achieve it
to start behaving in a better way; to change for the better
the result of chance rather than planning or skill

quizExercises.sentence: She ___ becoming an architect from the moment she first visited the city.
options: turned over a new leaf about
had no rhyme or reason for
quizExercises.explanation: 'Set your heart on' expresses a strong, emotionally driven desire to achieve something specific.

quizExercises.sentence: The new scheduling system seemed to have ___ – nobody could work out the logic.
quizExercises.explanation: 'No rhyme or reason' means there is no logical explanation or discernible pattern behind something.

quizExercises.idiom: on the spur of the moment
quizExercises.question: Which scenario best illustrates acting 'on the spur of the moment'?
options: A traveller books a flight six months in advance after researching destinations for weeks
A person hears a band playing in the street and spontaneously buys a ticket to their concert that evening
A manager follows a carefully prepared restructuring plan step by step
quizExercises.explanation: 'On the spur of the moment' describes an action taken immediately and without prior planning, prompted by an immediate feeling or situation.

quizExercises.question: Which of the following shows someone 'turning over a new leaf'?
options: A student who used to miss deadlines starts submitting all work on time after a serious conversation with their tutor
A professional who has always been organised decides to try a new filing system
A person returns to a bad habit after trying to give it up once
quizExercises.explanation: 'Turn over a new leaf' means making a genuine change for the better in one's behaviour, as shown in option A.
